Definition:
A document that formally authorizes the
existence of a project.
Purpose:
Provides the PM authority to
use organizational resources.
Common Problems:
No clear authority; project may be
seen as "unauthorized."

Definition:
A justification for the project based on cost,
benefits, and risks.
Purpose:
To prove the project is worth
the investment.
Common Problems:
Money may be wasted on projects
with no ROI.

Definition:
A list identifying all people or groups interested
in the project.
Purpose:
To track influence and
communication needs.
Common Problems:
Key people might be ignored,
leading to resistance.

Definition:
A detailed description of project deliverables
and boundaries.
Purpose:
To define what is (and isn't)
included in the project.
Common Problems:
"Scope creep" where work grows
uncontrollably.

Definition:
A hierarchical decomposition of the total scope
of work.
Purpose:
To break down complex tasks
into manageable chunks.
Common Problems:
Tasks are missed; poor cost and
time estimation.

Definition:
The master document defining how the project is
executed and controlled.
Purpose:
To provide a baseline for
progress and decision-making.
Common Problems:
Lack of direction; team works in
silos.

Definition:
A visual timeline showing tasks, durations, and
dependencies.
Purpose:
To track the schedule and see
task overlaps.
Common Problems:
Deadlines are missed; team
doesn't know what's next.

Definition:
A log of potential "what-if" scenarios and their
response plans.
Purpose:
To identify and mitigate threats
before they happen.
Common Problems:
Project is blindsided by predictable
problems.

Definition:
A chart showing who is Responsible,
Accountable, Consulted, and Informed.
Purpose:
To clarify roles and avoid
confusion over task ownership.
Common Problems:
Duplicate work or tasks "falling
through the cracks."

Definition:
A formal proposal to modify any document,
deliverable, or baseline.
Purpose:
To ensure changes are
evaluated for impact on
cost/time.
Common Problems:
Uncontrolled changes lead to
budget and time overruns.

Definition:
A record of current problems affecting the
project.
Purpose:
To document, track, and resolve
active hurdles.
Common Problems:
Problems are forgotten and
escalate into disasters.

Definition:
A regular update on the current health of the
project.
Purpose:
To keep stakeholders informed
on progress vs. plan.
Common Problems:
Stakeholders lose trust or lack
visibility.

Definition:
Standards and criteria the project must meet to
be successful.
Purpose:
To ensure the final product is
fit for use.
Common Problems:
High defect rates and client
dissatisfaction.

Definition:
A schedule showing when specific resources
(people/equip) are available.
Purpose:
To avoid over-allocating people
or tools.
Common Problems:
Resource conflicts; team members
are overworked.

Definition:
A description of work to be done by an outside
vendor.
Purpose:
To clearly define what a
contractor must deliver.
Common Problems:
Disputes with vendors over what
was "promised."

Definition: A list of significant points or events in a project.
Purpose:
To show high-level progress
without getting lost in details.
Common Problems:
Hard to judge if the project is
"on track" overall.

Definition:
A strategy for who gets what info, when,
and how.
Purpose:
To ensure the right people are
always informed.
Common Problems:
Information silos; stakeholders
feel left out.

Definition:
The sequence of tasks that determines the
shortest project duration.
Purpose:
To identify tasks that cannot be
delayed without delaying
the project.
Common Problems:
Inability to prioritize tasks that
impact the end date.

Definition:
A document recording what went well and what
didn't.
Purpose:
To improve performance in
future projects.
Common Problems:
Repeating the same mistakes in
the next project.

Definition:
A formal document confirming the project is
finished.
Purpose:
To handover deliverables and
release resources.
Common Problems:
Projects "drag on" indefinitely
without a clean end.

Definition:
An ordered list of everything that might
be needed in the product.
Purpose:
To serve as the single source of
requirements for any changes.
Common Problems:
Team lacks a clear priority of what
to build next.

Definition:
A set of backlog items selected for a specific
Sprint (short work cycle).
Purpose:
To define the work the team
commits to doing in the next
2-4 weeks.
Common Problems:
Lack of focus during the work
cycle; over-commitment.

Definition:
A graphic representation of work left to do
versus time.
Purpose:
To track the progress of a sprint
and predict if work will finish.
Common Problems:
Team won't realize they are
behind until the deadline hits.

Definition:
A measure of the amount of work a team
can tackle during a single period.
Purpose:
To help in planning future
capacity and timelines.
Common Problems:
Unrealistic scheduling and
"guess-work" planning.

Definition:
A measure of the financial effectiveness of a
project ($EV / AC$).
Purpose:
To see if the project is under or
over budget.
Common Problems:
Financial "bleeding" goes
unnoticed until funds run out.

Definition: A measure of schedule efficiency ($EV / PV$).
Purpose:
To see if the project is ahead of
or behind schedule.
Common Problems:
Delays are not quantified,
making recovery difficult.

Definition:
A record of all factors believed to be true without
proof.
Purpose:
To track variables that could
become risks if proven false.
Common Problems:
Decisions are made based on
"guesses" that may fail.

Definition:
Specifically defined attributes to be measured
(e.g., failure rate).
Purpose:
To provide an objective way to
measure "success."
Common Problems:
Quality is subjective; arguments
over "finished" work.

Definition:
A grid showing project resources assigned to
each work package.
Purpose:
To ensure every task has a
body assigned to it.
Common Problems:
Resources are under-utilized or
double-booked.

Definition:
The practice of comparing planned results with
actual results.
Purpose:
To identify why the project is
deviating from the plan.
Common Problems:
No understanding of why the
project is failing or succeeding.

Definition:
A document outlining the first formal meeting
with all stakeholders.
Purpose:
To align everyone on goals and
set the tone for the project.
Common Problems:
Confusion from day one; lack of
team cohesion.

Definition:
A document that solicits proposals from vendors
for a solution.
Purpose:
To compare different vendors'
solutions and pricing fairly.
Common Problems:
Biased vendor selection or
overpaying for services.

Definition:
A contract between a service provider and a
customer.
Purpose:
To define the level of service
(uptime, speed) expected.
Common Problems:
No legal recourse if the service
provider performs poorly.

Definition:
A shared understanding of what it means for
work to be complete.
Purpose:
To ensure quality and prevent
"half-baked" deliverables.
Common Problems:
Tasks are marked "done" but still
require bug fixes later.

Definition:
The original approved version of the project
schedule or budget.
Purpose:
To serve as the "yardstick" for
measuring performance.
Common Problems:
No way to know if you are over
budget or late.

Definition:
Informal, natural language descriptions of
features from the end-user's view.
Purpose:
To capture functional
requirements in a way that
provides value.
Common Problems:
Features are built that the
customer doesn't actually want.

Definition:
A bar chart showing the amount of time a
resource is scheduled.
Purpose:
To visually identify resource
overloads or "burnout."
Common Problems:
Team members are overworked
while others sit idle.

Definition:
A grid mapping the likelihood and severity
of risks.
Purpose:
To prioritize which risks need
immediate attention.
Common Problems:
Waste of time focusing on
"unlikely" or "minor" risks.

Definition:
A list of all changes submitted and their current
status.
Purpose:
To keep a history of how the
project evolved from the original
plan.
Common Problems:
No record of why the budget or
scope increased.

Definition:
A formal sign-off from the customer on the end
product.
Purpose:
To legally and professionally
close the project and stop work.
Common Problems:
The client keeps asking for "one
more thing" forever.

Definition:
A tool to document Suppliers, Inputs, Process,
Outputs, and Customers.
Purpose:
To provide a high-level view of
a process before detailing it.
Common Problems:
Misunderstanding of where the
project boundaries start/end.

Definition:
A visualization tool to categorize potential causes
of a problem.
Purpose:
To identify the root cause of an
issue rather than just symptoms.
Common Problems:
Solving the wrong problem; issues
keep recurring.

Definition:
A computerized mathematical technique to
account for risk in quantitative analysis.
Purpose:
To predict the probability of
completing a project on a
specific date.
Common Problems:
Unrealistic confidence in a single
"best-case" date.

Definition:
The lowest level of the Work
Breakdown Structure (WBS).
Purpose:
To define specific tasks that
can be estimated and managed.
Common Problems:
Work is too vague to assign or
estimate accurately.

Definition:
A visual workflow tool that uses cards to
represent work items.
Purpose:
To manage work-in-progress
(WIP) and optimize flow.
Common Problems:
Work gets "stuck" in bottlenecks
without being noticed.

Definition:
Key areas that must go right for the project to be
a success.
Purpose:
To focus the team's energy on
the most important variables.
Common Problems:
Efforts are wasted on "nice-to-have"
features that don't matter.

Definition:
Budget or time set aside for "known-unknowns"
(identified risks).
Purpose:
To handle risks that were
predicted in the Risk Register.
Common Problems:
Project runs out of money as soon
as a single risk occurs.

Definition:
Budget set aside for "unknown-unknowns"
(unforeseen events).
Purpose:
To provide a safety net for
totally unexpected disasters.
Common Problems:
Project is cancelled if a random,
unpredicted crisis hits.

Definition:
A bar graph where values are plotted in
decreasing order of frequency.
Purpose:
To follow the 80/20 rule (focus
on the 20% of causes creating
80% of problems).
Common Problems:
Fixing minor issues while the
biggest failures are ignored.

Definition:
The value of work actually performed expressed
in terms of the budget.
Purpose:
To measure the "true" progress
of a project vs. money spent.
Common Problems:
Thinking you are "under budget"
just because you haven't spent
much.

Definition:
A high-level visual summary that maps out the
vision of a product over time.
Purpose:
To communicate the long-term
strategy and direction.
Common Problems:
The team focuses on immediate
tasks and loses the "big picture."

Definition:
A narrative description of products, services, or
results to be delivered.
Purpose:
To define the technical and
performance requirements
of a contract.
Common Problems:
Legal disputes over what was
supposed to be built.

Definition: A tracking of velocity over multiple sprints.
Purpose:
To see if the team is becoming
more or less efficient over time.
Common Problems:
Inability to forecast long-term
release dates for the product.

Definition:
Notes from a meeting where the team discusses
how to improve.
Purpose:
To implement process
improvements immediately.
Common Problems:
The team continues to work in an
inefficient or toxic way.

Definition:
An assessment of the practicality of a proposed
plan or method.
Purpose:
To determine if a project is
technically or financially
possible.
Common Problems:
Investing millions into a project
that is impossible to finish.

Definition:
A technique in which start/finish dates are
adjusted based on resource constraints.
Purpose:
To ensure no one person is
assigned 60 hours of work in
a 40-hour week.
Common Problems: High staff turnover and burnout.

Definition:
A list of tasks that rely on other tasks or external
factors.
Purpose:
To ensure "Task B" doesn't start
before "Task A" is finished.
Common Problems:
Team members sit idle waiting for
others to finish work.

Definition:
A plan defining when and how to move a
problem to higher management.
Purpose:
To ensure big problems get the
attention they need quickly.
Common Problems:
Junior staff try to fix massive
problems alone, wasting time.

Definition:
A plan to ensure the project benefits are actually
achieved after closing.
Purpose:
To track if the project actually
improved the business as
intended.
Common Problems:
The project is "finished,"
but the business never sees
the profit.

Definition:
A set of conditions that must be met before a
deliverable is accepted.
Purpose:
To remove ambiguity regarding
"quality" or "completion."
Common Problems:
Rejection of work by the client
at the very last minute.

Definition:
A lean tool used to document every step in a
process from start to finish.
Purpose:
To identify and eliminate non-
value-added activities (waste).
Common Problems:
The project team remains
inefficient without knowing where
the lag is.

Definition:
A forecast of project costs to be paid that
includes the "when" and "how much."
Purpose:
To ensure the organization has
the cash flow ready for project
bills.
Common Problems:
Project stops abruptly because
there is no cash to pay vendors.

Definition:
A sensitivity analysis tool showing which risks
have the most impact.
Purpose:
To visually prioritize the risks
that could swing the budget the
most.
Common Problems:
Focus is spread thin across all
risks instead of the "big hitters."

Definition:
A technique to categorize requirements: Must
have, Should have, Could have, Won't have.
Purpose:
To manage scope when time or
budget is limited.
Common Problems:
Everything is treated as "urgent,"
leading to total project burnout.

Definition:
The rate at which the remaining work is
decreasing.
Purpose:
To predict the exact day the
current workload will hit zero.
Common Problems:
Management sets unrealistic
launch dates based on hope,
not data.

Definition:
Adjustments to the start/end of dependent tasks
(Lead accelerates, Lag delays).
Purpose:
To fine-tune the schedule for
realistic transitions between
tasks.
Common Problems:
The schedule is too rigid or
physically impossible to execute.

Definition:
A contract type where the price is set and does
not change.
Purpose:
To shift the financial risk onto
the seller/vendor.
Common Problems:
Cost overruns by the vendor
become the buyer's financial
problem.

Definition:
A contract where the buyer pays for costs plus a
fixed fee.
Purpose:
To encourage vendors to take
on high-risk, experimental work.
Common Problems:
No incentive for the vendor to
control costs; budget may spiral.

Definition:
A management control point where scope, cost,
and schedule are integrated.
Purpose:
To track performance at a level
above individual work packages.
Common Problems:
Hard to pinpoint which specific
department is overspending.

Definition:
A real-time visual display of Key Performance
Indicators (KPIs).
Purpose:
To provide an "at-a-glance"
health check of the project.
Common Problems:
Stakeholders have to dig through
50-page reports to find basic info.

Definition:
A technique used for constructing a schedule
model (Boxes and Arrows).
Purpose:
To visualize how all tasks are
logically connected.
Common Problems:
Logic errors in the schedule
(e.g., building the roof before
the walls).

Definition:
Supporting documentation explaining how
cost/time figures were calculated.
Purpose:
To provide transparency and
"defend" the budget to the
board.
Common Problems:
The budget looks like
"made-up numbers" and gets
cut by management.

Definition:
A structured review of the procurement process
from start to finish.
Purpose:
To identify successes and
failures in vendor management.
Common Problems:
Legal loopholes or bad vendor
behaviors are repeated in next
project.

Definition:
The software or tools used to gather and
distribute information.
Purpose:
To centralize all project data
(e.g., Jira, MS Project, Asana).
Common Problems:
Information is lost in email threads
and personal spreadsheets.

Definition: A formal procedure for sanctioning project work.
Purpose:
To ensure work is done at the
right time and in the right
sequence.
Common Problems:
Team members start tasks too
early, wasting resources.

Definition:
The rules, roles, and processes that guide
project activities.
Purpose:
To ensure the project aligns
with the company’s strategic
goals.
Common Problems:
The project is successful
technically but fails the
company's mission.

Definition:
A document detailing how the "end product"
moves to the "end user."
Purpose:
To ensure the project isn't
"dropped" on users without
training.
Common Problems:
The product is built but nobody
knows how to maintain or use it.

Definition:
A legal agreement between two entities to work
together on a project.
Purpose:
To define the partnership
before a full contract is signed.
Common Problems:
Partners fight over intellectual
property or profit sharing later.

Definition:
A grid that links requirements from their origin to
the deliverables.
Purpose:
To ensure that every single
requirement is actually tested
and met.
Common Problems:
Features are missed during
development, failing the client's
needs.

Definition:
The practice of adding extra features not
requested by the client.
Purpose:
(Often an error) Trying to "wow"
the client with unrequested work.
Common Problems:
Wasted budget and time on things
the client didn't ask for.

Definition:
A high-level visual of all active projects within an
organization.
Purpose:
To ensure the mix of projects
balances risk and strategic
reward.
Common Problems:
The company takes on too many
high-risk projects at once.

Definition:
A projection of the budget deficit or surplus at the
end of the project.
Purpose:
To warn stakeholders exactly
how much the project will be
over/under budget.
Common Problems:
Financial surprises at the very
end of the project when it's too
late.

Definition:
An audit to ensure the organization is ready to
support the new product.
Purpose:
To confirm that support staff,
manuals, and tools are in place
for launch.
Common Problems:
The project "goes live" but the
support team is unable to help
users.

Definition:
An evaluation conducted months after the
project is closed.
Purpose:
To measure if the project actually
delivered the expected long-term
value.
Common Problems:
No one knows if the project
actually solved the original
business problem.

Definition:
A legal document stating the work is substantially
complete and safe for use.
Purpose:
To hand over the responsibility/
liability of the site or product
to the owner.
Common Problems:
Ongoing legal liability for the
project team even after they
have left.
